result.markdown:
--- title: "What if UEFA qualifying used a Swiss system?" subtitle: "Simulating a fairer road to the World Cup with a hierarchical Bayesian model" description: > UEFA's group-stage qualifiers can hand two equally good teams very different fates. I rebuilt qualifying as a Swiss-system tournament, powered it with a Bayesian Poisson model, and ran it ten thousand times to ask a simple question: would it be fairer? author: "Matteo Morella" date: "2026-01-01" # <-- aggiorna alla data di pubblicazione categories: [football, bayesian, simulation, tournament-design] format: html: toc: true toc-depth: 2 code-fold: true code-tools: true fig-cap-location: bottom execute: echo: true warning: false freeze: auto bibliography: references.bib --- ::: callout-note This is a companion write-up to a longer methodological paper. Here I keep the maths light and let the charts do the arguing. The full model specification, priors, and convergence diagnostics live in the paper. All the code and data are on [GitHub](https://github.com/mat126/bayesian-uefa-swiss). ::: ## The complaint nobody can prove Every qualifying cycle some country misses out by a whisker and their fans spend a year calling it unlucky. I think the instinct is right but the target is wrong. People blame the draw, as if they were handed an impossibly hard group, when the pot system makes a genuinely brutal group nearly impossible to assemble in the first place. The real problem with group-stage qualifying is structural, and it has less to do with who is in your group than with how little room the format leaves you to recover. A qualifying group is short. You play a small handful of opponents, home and away, and that hands every single match an enormous weight. Drop points once, in a game you were expected to win, and you can spend the rest of the campaign chasing a gap that never closes, because there are barely any matches left in which to close it. One bad night, and the whole qualification is compromised. Seeding is supposed to keep things balanced by spreading strength evenly across the groups, and mostly it does. When a group does turn out lopsided, it is usually not bad luck at all but a seeding failure: a team that is genuinely strong gets slotted into a lower pot, because the pots lean on rankings that do not always reflect real quality. And the pots are locked in once, months before anyone kicks a ball. Two teams of similar ability can still end up with schedules of very different difficulty, and neither of them had any say in it. A Swiss system takes aim at both problems at once. After the opening round you are always paired against teams on your own points total, so you spend the tournament playing opponents at your level rather than whoever the pots happened to assign. A single slip stops being fatal: lose, and you drop into matches you can win, with your final position reflecting a full run of balanced games instead of one costly result. So here is the question I set out to answer. If UEFA ran qualifying as a **Swiss-system tournament** instead of fixed groups, how much would the outcomes change, and would the format really be *fairer*? ## The idea: borrow the format from chess A Swiss system does something groups cannot. Instead of locking your opponents in before the tournament starts, it pairs you round by round against teams on your own points total, with each round's matchups decided only once the previous one is over. Win early and you climb into harder matchups; stumble and you drop into easier ones. Nobody is ever quarantined in a "group of death," because there are no groups and no fixed draw. The field self-sorts as it goes. Chess has used this for over a century to rank hundreds of players in a handful of rounds, and the idea is no longer foreign to football's own governance either. In 2024 UEFA abolished the Champions League group stage and replaced it with a single 36-team league table, a change it marketed as a "Swiss system." But it is only half a Swiss system. The opponents are still drawn up front from seeded pots, before a ball is kicked, and the full schedule is fixed from day one. What UEFA borrowed was the single-table structure, not the round-by-round pairing that makes a Swiss system fair, the part where matchups adapt to results as they happen instead of being locked in by a seeding that, as we have seen, does not always get the pecking order right. UEFA left that part on the table, and it is exactly the part I want to put back. The appeal, for a fairness question, is what that pairing does over a full tournament: it makes each team's schedule track its own level. You end up facing opponents at your strength almost by construction, so the difficulty of your road depends on how you play rather than on where the draw happened to place you. That is precisely the property the group format cannot guarantee, and precisely the one I set out to measure. ## Building the tournament engine The simulator handles the 54 eligible UEFA nations (Russia is suspended) over eight rounds. The mechanics that matter: - **Swiss pairings** with no rematches. With an even field of 54 teams every round pairs cleanly, so no byes are needed. - **Home/away balancing**, so no team is stuck playing everyone away. - **Geopolitical restrictions**, so pairings that cannot happen in reality (Spain–Gibraltar, Armenia–Azerbaijan, Kosovo–Serbia) are avoided. - A standard tiebreak stack: points, wins, Sonneborn-Berger, Buchholz, goal difference, goals for, FIFA ranking. The one thing the engine does not decide on its own is the result of each match. That is a plug-in. The simulator asks a probability function for the odds of a home win, draw, and away win, and simulates from there: ::: {#392545b9 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} def prob_fn(home_team, away_team) -> tuple[float, float, float]: # returns (p_home_win, p_draw, p_away_win) ... SwissTournament(teams=teams, prob_fn=prob_fn).run() ``` ::: A placeholder version of that function just uses the FIFA ranking gap. It is fine for testing the plumbing but a weak forecaster. The interesting work is in what fills the slot, which I will come to. First, a problem that has nothing to do with the model and everything to do with how you decide who qualifies. ## Qualifying, and the problem with a hard cut line Say twelve nations qualify directly for the World Cup and the rest fight through a play-off. The obvious way to run this is to rank all 54 teams at the end and draw a line: the top twelve go straight through, and a band below them enters the play-off. Clean on paper. In practice the line lands in a terrible place. In an eight-round Swiss the points pile up in narrow bands, and near the cut they barely separate at all. Across 10\,000 simulations the twelfth-placed team averages np.float64(14.2) points and the thirteenth np.float64(14.0), a gap of a quarter of a point. More to the point: ::: {#cell-tie-stat .cell execution_count=3} ::: {#tie-stat .cell-output .cell-output-display .cell-output-markdown execution_count=2} In **77%** of simulations the team that qualifies directly and the team that just misses finish level on points. The cut does not separate a stronger team from a weaker one. It separates two teams with the same record, on goal difference or a tiebreak, and sends one to the World Cup and the other to a play-off. ::: ::: That is the arbitrariness the whole exercise was meant to remove, sneaking back in at the last step. And it reaches surprisingly far up the table. The tied band around the cut is usually four or five teams wide, so a side sitting as high as tenth can share the cut's points total: it happens in almost half of all simulations (46%). Drawing a hard line at position twelve would send some of those teams through and drag others down, on nothing more than goal difference. ::: {#fig-points-cut} ::: {#cell-fig-points-cut-code .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} import plotly.express as px band = hist[hist["pos"].between(8, 20)] fig = px.box(band, x="pos", y="points", labels={"pos": "Final position", "points": "Points"}) fig.add_vline(x=12.5, line_dash="dash", line_color="crimson") fig.update_layout(showlegend=False, margin=dict(t=10)) fig.show() ``` ::: {#fig-points-cut-code .cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Points by final position across all simulations, for the positions around the direct-qualification cut. The dashed line sits after twelfth place. Notice how the boxes on either side of it overlap almost completely: a hard cut here separates teams that are, on points, indistinguishable. ::: ::: ::: The fix is not mine to invent, because formats that run Swiss into a play-off have lived with this for years and solved it. In competitive Pokémon (the VGC circuit) and in chess, the cut to the next stage is not a rank, it is a record. Everyone who finishes with at most a set number of losses advances, and the bracket that follows is **seeded by final standing**. The line is drawn between record tiers, never between two tied competitors. Ported to qualifying, that gives a cleaner design. Direct qualification is reserved for the top tier, the teams clearly clear of the contested band. Everyone in that band drops into the play-off, but they carry their standing with them as a seed. That seeding is the whole point. A tenth-placed team pulled down by a points tie does not enter the play-off as an afterthought; it enters as a top seed, which in football terms means a home tie against the weakest side to reach the play-off. It is dragged in, but it is dragged in holding the best hand. This keeps faith with the premise the format started from. One result no longer flips you from *in* to *out*. It moves you from a direct ticket to a favourable play-off, and where you finished still counts, now as a seed rather than as a coin toss on goal difference. Exactly how many teams qualify directly, how wide the play-off tier runs, and how many play-off winners go through are all design levers I can turn. What matters is the principle: the cut should respect tiers, not slice through teams that finished level. ## The engine under the hood: a Bayesian Poisson model ::: callout-tip ## You can skip this section If you only care about the tournament results, jump ahead. This is the part that turns "team A plays team B" into a credible score distribution. ::: Football scores are counts, so a Poisson model is the natural starting point. Each team gets an attacking strength and a defensive strength, and the expected goals in a match come from the attack of one side against the defence of the other, plus a home advantage term: $$ \log(\lambda_{ij}) = \mu + \text{att}_i + \text{def}_j + \gamma \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{home}] $$ This is the lineage of @maher1982 and @dixoncoles1997, made Bayesian and hierarchical after @baioblangiardo2010. Two design choices matter. The strengths are **partially pooled**, so thin-data teams like San Marino are pulled toward the population mean rather than fit wildly on a handful of lopsided results, and the likelihood carries an **exponential time decay**, so last autumn's form counts for more than a match from four years ago, with friendlies downweighted against competitive fixtures. The result is one attack and one defence parameter per nation. ::: {#fig-team-params} ::: {#cell-fig-team-params-code .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} fig = px.scatter(params, x="Alpha", y="Beta", text="Squadra", labels={"Alpha": "Attack (α)", "Beta": "Defence (β)"}) fig.update_traces(textposition="top center", marker=dict(size=7)) fig.update_layout(margin=dict(t=10)) fig.show() ``` ::: {#fig-team-params-code .cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Posterior attack (α) and defence (β) parameters for each nation. Sign conventions follow the model above; read positions relative to the cloud rather than as absolute quality. ::: ::: ::: Before trusting any of it I check that the model predicts real matches well. The chart below bins held-out predictions and plots the predicted probability against the observed frequency of each outcome; a well-calibrated model sits on the diagonal. ::: {#fig-calibration} ::: {#cell-fig-calibration-code .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} import plotly.graph_objects as go fig = go.Figure() edges = np.linspace(0, 1, 11) for col, out, name in [("pred_home", "H", "Home win"), ("pred_draw", "D", "Draw"), ("pred_away", "A", "Away win")]: cal["_bin"] = pd.cut(cal[col], bins=edges) grp = (cal.groupby("_bin", observed=True) .apply(lambda d: pd.Series({"pred": d[col].mean(), "obs": (d["observed"] == out).mean()})) .dropna()) fig.add_scatter(x=grp["pred"], y=grp["obs"], mode="markers+lines", name=name) fig.add_scatter(x=[0, 1], y=[0, 1], mode="lines", line=dict(dash="dash", color="grey"), showlegend=False) fig.update_layout(xaxis_title="Predicted probability", yaxis_title="Observed frequency", margin=dict(t=10)) fig.show() ``` ::: {#fig-calibration-code .cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Calibration on held-out matches. Points on the diagonal mean the model's stated probabilities match reality. ::: ::: ::: ::: callout-note ## Validation On 483 held-out matches the model scores a Ranked Probability Score of **0\.160**, against **0\.230** for a baseline that always predicts the historical frequency of a home win, draw, and away win. Lower is better, so the model cuts the naive error by roughly 30%. Sampling converged cleanly, with all R-hat at 1.00 and no divergent transitions. ::: The point to keep straight: I validate the *model* on matches that exist, because a Swiss qualifying campaign has never been played and never can be observed. Once the model earns trust on real football, I use it as the engine to project a format that does not. ## Running it ten thousand times A single simulated campaign is one story. To learn anything I need the distribution of stories, so the model feeds the simulator and the whole tournament runs under Monte Carlo, recording where each nation finishes every time. ::: {#5ca05724 .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} results = [] for _ in range(N_SIMULATIONS): tournament = SwissTournament(teams=fresh_teams(), prob_fn=bayesian_prob_fn) results.append(tournament.run(verbose_rounds=False)) ``` ::: I ran 10\,000 simulations, enough that each nation's qualification probability is stable to well within a percentage point. ## What the Swiss system produces Here is each nation's chance of qualifying directly and of reaching the play-off. ::: {#fig-qual-probs} ::: {#cell-fig-qual-probs-code .cell execution_count=9} ``` {.python .cell-code} top = mc.sort_values("P(qualif.)") fig = go.Figure() fig.add_bar(y=top["Squadra"], x=top["P(diretta)"], name="Direct", orientation="h") fig.add_bar(y=top["Squadra"], x=top["P(playoff)"], name="Play-off", orientation="h") fig.update_layout(barmode="stack", xaxis_title="Probability", yaxis_title="", legend_title="", height=900, margin=dict(t=10)) fig.show() ``` ::: {#fig-qual-probs-code .cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Probability of direct qualification and of reaching the play-off, by nation. ::: ::: ::: No surprises at the extremes. The strongest sides qualify in almost every simulation and the weakest almost never do. The story lives in the middle, among the teams whose fate is genuinely in play, and that is where a change of format has room to matter. ::: {#fig-interactive-positions} ::: {#cell-fig-interactive-positions-code .cell execution_count=10} ``` {.python .cell-code} import numpy as np import plotly.graph_objects as go DIRECT_SPOTS = 12 PLAYOFF_SPOTS = 16 n_total = hist["team"].nunique() # 54 nazionali # ── Conteggi per squadra e posizione (righe = squadre, colonne = 1..54) ── pos_counts = (hist.groupby(["team", "pos"]).size() .unstack("pos", fill_value=0) .reindex(columns=range(1, n_total + 1), fill_value=0)) # ── Statistiche di sintesi per squadra ────────────────────────────────── summ = hist.groupby("team").agg(mean_w=("wins", "mean"), mean_d=("draws", "mean"), mean_l=("losses", "mean"), med=("pos", "median")) summ["p_dir"] = pos_counts.loc[:, 1:DIRECT_SPOTS].sum(axis=1) / N_SIMS * 100 summ["p_play"] = (pos_counts.loc[:, DIRECT_SPOTS + 1:DIRECT_SPOTS + PLAYOFF_SPOTS] .sum(axis=1) / N_SIMS * 100) # Ordino le squadre per probabilità di qualificazione diretta (decrescente) sorted_teams = summ.sort_values("p_dir", ascending=False).index.tolist() # ── Colori per fascia: verde = diretta, arancio = playoff, grigio = fuori ─ COLORS = (["#2e7d32"] * DIRECT_SPOTS + ["#ef6c00"] * PLAYOFF_SPOTS + ["#bdbdbd"] * (n_total - DIRECT_SPOTS - PLAYOFF_SPOTS)) def _title(team): """Titolo dinamico con le statistiche chiave della squadra selezionata.""" s = summ.loc[team] return (f"{team} – Distribuzione posizioni finali
" f"" f"✓ Diretta: {s.p_dir:.1f}% | " f"○ Playoff: {s.p_play:.1f}% | " f"Mediana: {int(s.med)}° | " f"Media V-P-S: {s.mean_w:.1f}–{s.mean_d:.1f}–{s.mean_l:.1f}" f"") # ── Una traccia a barre per squadra, visibile solo la prima all'avvio ──── fig = go.Figure() for i, team in enumerate(sorted_teams): counts = pos_counts.loc[team].to_numpy() fig.add_trace(go.Bar( x=list(range(1, n_total + 1)), y=counts.tolist(), name=team, visible=(i == 0), marker_color=COLORS, customdata=(counts / N_SIMS * 100).tolist(), hovertemplate=("Posizione %{x}
%{y} simulazioni
" "%{customdata:.1f}% dei casi"), )) # ── Dropdown: ogni voce mostra una sola traccia e aggiorna il titolo ───── buttons = [] for i, team in enumerate(sorted_teams): buttons.append(dict( label=team, method="update", args=[{"visible": [j == i for j in range(len(sorted_teams))]}, {"title": {"text": _title(team), "font": {"size": 15}}}], )) fig.update_layout( title=dict(text=_title(sorted_teams[0]), font=dict(size=15)), updatemenus=[dict(type="dropdown", x=0.0, y=1.22, xanchor="left", yanchor="top", buttons=buttons, showactive=True, bgcolor="#eef2ff", bordercolor="#cccccc")], annotations=[dict(text="Squadra:", x=0.0, y=1.27, xref="paper", yref="paper", showarrow=False, font=dict(size=13))], xaxis=dict(title="Posizione finale", dtick=5, gridcolor="#eeeeee"), yaxis=dict(title="Simulazioni", gridcolor="#eeeeee"), plot_bgcolor="white", paper_bgcolor="white", height=500, margin=dict(t=130, b=60, l=70, r=30), shapes=[ # Linea qualificazione diretta (dopo la 12ª) dict(type="line", x0=DIRECT_SPOTS + 0.5, x1=DIRECT_SPOTS + 0.5, y0=0, y1=1, yref="paper", line=dict(color="#1a1a1a", width=2)), # Linea limite playoff (dopo la 28ª) dict(type="line", x0=DIRECT_SPOTS + PLAYOFF_SPOTS + 0.5, x1=DIRECT_SPOTS + PLAYOFF_SPOTS + 0.5, y0=0, y1=1, yref="paper", line=dict(color="#888888", width=1.5, dash="dash")), ], ) fig # in Quarto basta questo: niente pio.write_html ``` ::: {#fig-interactive-positions-code .cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Distribuzione delle posizioni finali per ogni nazionale su tutte le simulazioni. Usa il menu a tendina per cambiare squadra. La linea nera segna la qualificazione diretta (12ª), quella tratteggiata il limite dei playoff. ::: ::: ::: ## Is it actually fairer? This is where "fairer" has to stop being a feeling and become a number. The cleanest version of the question is whether the format rewards being good. If it is fair, a team's chance of qualifying should rise steadily with its underlying strength, and little else should get in the way. ::: {#fig-strength-qual} ::: {#cell-fig-strength-qual-code .cell execution_count=11} ``` {.python .cell-code} fig = px.scatter(sq, x="alpha", y="p_qual", text="team", labels={"alpha": "Estimated strength (posterior α)", "p_qual": "P(qualify)"}) fig.update_traces(textposition="top center", marker=dict(size=8)) fig.update_layout(margin=dict(t=10)) fig.show() ``` ::: {#fig-strength-qual-code .cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Estimated attacking strength against probability of qualifying, one point per nation. The tighter and steeper this relationship, the more the format sorts teams by merit rather than by luck. ::: ::: ::: ## Where this breaks down I would rather tell you the limits than have you find them. The market-value features are a current snapshot, not a match-by-match history, so a team's estimated quality does not travel back in time correctly. Team strengths are also static within a campaign: real form drifts over a qualifying cycle and the model does not follow that drift. And the deepest caveat is unavoidable. I am simulating a world in which 54 nations play a format they have never played. The model is trained on the football that exists, and I am asking it to extrapolate to football that does not. That is a reasonable thing to do, but it is an extrapolation, and the fairness conclusions are conditional on it. ## Takeaways Two things come out of this cleanly. The model earns its keep, scoring well below a naive baseline on matches it never saw, and a Swiss qualifying campaign sorts the nations largely in order of their strength: qualification probability rises smoothly with estimated ability, which is the basic thing you want a fair format to do. The strongest sides get through, the weakest do not, and not much in between hangs on an accident of the draw. The sharper lesson is smaller than the one I set out to find. A Swiss system does not hand you fairness for free. Run naively it smuggles back the very arbitrariness it was meant to remove, because points bunch so tightly that the qualifying line almost always falls between teams that finished level. Borrowing the record-tier cut and seeded play-off from chess and competitive Pokémon repairs that, and keeps faith with the premise the whole thing rests on: one bad night should cost you a seed, not your place. Whether all of this adds up to a format that is genuinely fairer than the group draw is the question I have not fully settled here. Sorting by merit is necessary but it is not the whole of fairness, and the honest head-to-head against the real groups, with a fairness measure I actually trust, is the next piece of work. For now the takeaway is narrower and, I think, more interesting than a verdict: a Swiss World Cup qualifier is buildable, it behaves sensibly, and its hardest problem turns out to be not the football but where you draw the line. The code, the model, and every chart in this post are reproducible from the [repository](https://github.com/mat126/bayesian-uefa-swiss). If you spot something wrong, I would like to know. ## References ::: {#refs} ::: ## References ::: {#refs} :::
